summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies NDAs by explaining that they rarely serve their intended purpose of preventing information leaks. He argues that NDAs are practically unenforceable in court because it's nearly impossible to prove someone leaked a specific secret. Instead, NDAs function primarily as tools for legal intimidation—a 'force multiplier in legal blustering.' The key dynamic governing whether NDAs get signed is leverage: whoever has the money or power can demand signatures. Shapiro advises entrepreneurs to stop asking investors to sign NDAs (it signals inexperience) and instead reserve NDA requests for situations where you hold the leverage.
key_points: NDAs are nearly unenforceable because proving someone leaked a secret rather than independently discovered it is extremely difficult
The primary practical function of NDAs is as a threat multiplier for potential lawsuits, not actual confidentiality protection
Whether an NDA gets signed comes down entirely to leverage—whoever controls the money or value in the relationship dictates terms
Asking investors to sign NDAs signals inexperience and lack of understanding of power dynamics in fundraising
Honorable people respect confidentiality without paperwork, and deceptive people ignore it regardless of what they sign
money_quotes: There is no love like a first time entrepreneur's love with nondisclosure agreements.
But that's a giant stinking load of donkey dung. It's almost never going to happen that you actually sue someone for disclosing a secret and prevail.
NDAs are a force multiplier in legal blustering.
I find deceptive folk make a habit of being deceptive, and honorable folk respect these things with or without paperwork.
There's one and only one reason that NDAs get signed: when one side or both have a great deal of leverage in the negotiation.
If you are asking someone for money, don't ask them to sign an NDA. You will come off looking like an ignoramus or a terrorist.
Real wants you to sign an NDA before they let you pee. This is not a dramatic hyperbole to emphasize their vigor in pursuing your signature; it's the literal truth.
key_insight: NDAs are not confidentiality tools but leverage instruments—they get signed when one party has power over the other, and asking for one when you lack leverage only signals inexperience.
